instruction:
You are acting as a platform engineer who has just finished modifying a set of Kubernetes manifests that live in
    /home/user/k8s/manifests.  
    The directory already exists and (for the purposes of this exercise) currently contains exactly three YAML files:

        /home/user/k8s/manifests/deployment.yaml
        /home/user/k8s/manifests/service.yaml
        /home/user/k8s/manifests/configmap.yaml

    Goal  
    ----  
    Create a gzip-compressed tar archive of the entire /home/user/k8s/manifests directory.  
       * The archive *must* be created at the absolute path /home/user/manifests_backup.tar.gz.  
       * The resulting tarball must include the manifests directory itself as the top-level item, so that when someone
         runs “tar -tzf /home/user/manifests_backup.tar.gz” the very first path listed is
         “k8s/manifests/”.  

    Immediately after creating the archive, append a single log line containing only the absolute path to the archive
       (i.e. “/home/user/manifests_backup.tar.gz”) to the file /home/user/backup.log.  
       * If /home/user/backup.log does not already exist, it must be created automatically.  
       * Do **not** add extra spaces, newline characters, timestamps or anything else—just the full path followed by a
         single trailing newline.  

    Output format requirements that automated tests will verify  
    -----------------------------------------------------------  
    1. The file /home/user/manifests_backup.tar.gz must exist.  
    2. Running “tar -tzf /home/user/manifests_backup.tar.gz” must list exactly these three entries (order does not
       matter, but the paths are case-sensitive):  
           k8s/manifests/deployment.yaml  
           k8s/manifests/service.yaml  
           k8s/manifests/configmap.yaml  
    3. The file /home/user/backup.log must exist and contain *exactly one* non-empty line:  

           /home/user/manifests_backup.tar.gz  

       (including the trailing newline). No other characters are allowed.

You are a cloud architect reviewing a legacy deployment. In /home/user/images_to_migrate.log you already have a plain-text list of Docker/OCI image tags, one tag per line. Your goal is to determine how many times each distinct image appears in that list and save the result to an output file. 1. Read the source file located at /home/user/images_to_migrate.log. 2. Produce a frequency report in a **new** file named /home/user/image_frequency.report. 3. The report must fulfil **all** of the following formatting rules so that automated grading can parse it precisely: • Each line describes one unique image tag. • The line must start with the absolute decimal occurrence count, followed by a single space, followed by the exact image tag text. • No leading or trailing whitespace is permitted on any line. • Lines must be ordered by descending count (largest count first). When two images have the same count, order them in ascending (A→Z) lexicographic order of the tag string. • Example of the required line shape (do **not** copy these numbers; they are illustrative only): 42 myorg/service:1.2.3 4. Do not modify the original /home/user/images_to_migrate.log file. 5. When you are done, you can inspect the contents of /home/user/image_frequency.report in the terminal so the grader can capture the final state, but make sure the file itself exists with the required content and exact filename.

You are an edge-computing engineer who has just received three new IoT devices that must be registered in an on-board SQLite database.  
Using only terminal commands:

*   Create the directory `/home/user/iot_edge` (if it does not already exist).  
*   Inside that directory create an SQLite database file called `iot_devices.db`.  
*   In that database add one table exactly named `device_info` with the following columns:
    *   `device_id`  INTEGER  PRIMARY KEY  
    *   `hostname`   TEXT     NOT NULL  
    *   `ip_address` TEXT     NOT NULL  
    *   `status`     TEXT     NOT NULL  
    *   `last_seen`  TIMESTAMP NOT NULL  

    Populate the table with the three rows shown below (device_id values must match exactly).
    ```
    --------------------------------------------------------------------------
    device_id | hostname      | ip_address     | status  | last_seen
    ----------+---------------+----------------+---------+-------------------
    1         | edge-node-01  | 192.168.10.21  | ONLINE  | 2023-08-18 10:40:00
    2         | edge-node-02  | 192.168.10.22  | OFFLINE | 2023-08-18 09:20:00
    3         | sensor-hub-01 | 192.168.10.31  | ONLINE  | 2023-08-18 10:35:00
    --------------------------------------------------------------------------
    ```
*   Run a query that selects only the offline devices (`status = 'OFFLINE'`) and write the exact query results—**including a one-line CSV header**—to the file `/home/user/iot_edge/offline_devices.log`

    The file MUST contain precisely the following two lines and nothing else:  
    ```
    device_id,hostname,status
    2,edge-node-02,OFFLINE
    ```

A quick checklist for you:  
*   Directory path:  `/home/user/iot_edge`  
*   SQLite file:     `/home/user/iot_edge/iot_devices.db`  
*   Log file:        `/home/user/iot_edge/offline_devices.log`  

The automated grader will verify:  
a. correct directory and file names;  
b. table schema and row contents;  
c. exact two-line CSV in `offline_devices.log`.

You are working as a compliance analyst who must create a simple, self-contained audit trail for an existing Git project that already contains a sub-module.
Your manager wants a **single, plain-text log file** that captures the *exact* console output of the three commands listed below, executed from inside the repository’s root directory **/home/user/project**.

Commands to capture (in this order):
  1. `git status --short`
  2. `git submodule status`
  3. `git rev-parse HEAD`

Output file details
  • Path: **/home/user/audit/git_audit_log.txt** (create the audit directory if it does not exist).
  • Encoding: UTF-8.
  • The file must follow the *precise* four-section template shown below.
  • Section headers start with two equals signs `==`, followed by a single space, the command, another single space, and a final pair of equals signs.
  • After every header line, reproduce the *verbatim* command output exactly as it appeared in your terminal session.
  • There is **one blank line** between sections, and the file ends with a trailing newline.

Template the automated tests will check (angle brackets show where your captured output goes, do not include them literally):

    == git status --short ==
    <verbatim output of git status --short>

    == git submodule status ==
    <verbatim output of git submodule status>

    == git rev-parse HEAD ==
    <verbatim output of git rev-parse HEAD>

Additional guidance
  • You do not need to modify, commit, or update anything in the repository; just run the commands and capture their outputs in the required format.
  • Do **not** hard-code the expected hashes; record exactly what the commands print in your current session.
  • Make sure the final file location and content can be verified automatically, so double-check path, spacing, blank lines, and the trailing newline.

When you are finished, `/home/user/audit/git_audit_log.txt` should exist and match the template above, containing the real command outputs from your environment.

A small collection of CSV files has already been placed in
    /home/user/data/raw/.  
    You are a machine–learning engineer who must prepare this information
    for a model-training pipeline.  
    Create three new artefacts and respect the *exact* directory / file
    names and the formats that follow ­– the automated grader will read
    them byte-for-byte:

    1. /home/user/data/combined/products_all.csv  
       • Must contain **every** row from both raw CSV files, with the same
         header line, and in the order “2021 rows first, then 2022 rows”.  
       • A final newline **is required**.

    2. /home/user/data/processed/products_clean.jsonl  
       • Build it from products_all.csv but  
         – drop every row whose discontinued column is "yes"  
         – convert product_name to lower-case  
         – keep **only** the four fields: id (int), product_name (string),
           price (float), category (string) – in that order.  
       • Each line must be a single JSON object (newline-delimited JSON).  
       • No extra spaces anywhere inside the JSON objects.  
       • A final newline **is required**.

       Example of one correct line (do **not** hard-code):  
       {"id":1,"product_name":"widget","price":19.99,"category":"gadgets"}

    3. /home/user/data/processed/prep.log  
       • Exactly four lines, each using the syntax  
         key: value  
         (keys and the colon are lowercase; one space after the colon).  
       • The first line MUST be  
         timestamp: 2023-04-01T12:00:00Z  
         (write it literally – do not calculate the date).  
       • The next three lines must be:  
         total_rows_original: <integer>  
         rows_after_filter: <integer>  
         unique_categories: <comma-separated-list-in-alphabetical-order>  
       • No extra blank lines, and a final newline **is required**.

    Remember: paths, filenames and the exact formats are strict – tests
    will fail on even a single character difference.

You are a data-analyst who wants to demonstrate simple Unix process-management while (pretending to) crunch a CSV file.

Initial setup available to you:
• A directory /home/user/data already exists.
• Inside it there is a file called sales.csv (small demo data; you do not have to read it).

Goal
1. Launch a single long-running command that “processes” sales.csv in the background.
   • The command must keep running long enough that you can view and then terminate it.
2. Immediately verify the process exists and identify its Process ID (PID).
3. Cleanly terminate that very same process.
4. Verify that it is no longer running.
5. Produce a short log summarising the lifecycle.

Required artefact
Create a plain-text log file at:
/home/user/process_log.txt

The file must have EXACTLY two lines, no blank lines before, after, or between, following this strict pattern (case-sensitive, single spaces as shown):

PID:<PID_NUMBER> STATUS:RUNNING
PID:<PID_NUMBER> STATUS:TERMINATED

Constraints and verification details:
• <PID_NUMBER> must be replaced with the numeric PID assigned to the background process you started. It must be identical on both lines.
• “STATUS:RUNNING” must reflect the moment right after you confirmed the process was active.
• “STATUS:TERMINATED” must reflect the moment right after you confirmed you had terminated it.
• The process must truly be gone when the log is produced; automated tests will ensure that no process with that PID is still present.

Only the file /home/user/process_log.txt should be created or modified; do not move or rename existing files.

Avoid sudo/root commands—your user does not have elevated rights.

When finished, double-check that:
• /home/user/process_log.txt exists.
• It contains exactly two lines in the format specified above.
• The PID listed is no longer running.

You may print console output while working, but the automated grader will evaluate only the correctness of /home/user/process_log.txt and the absence of the process.

You are a storage-systems administrator who has just been told that the company’s backup appliance will start exporting data over two network-storage protocols: NFS (TCP 2049) and iSCSI (TCP 3260).
Before the appliance is connected, you have to prepare a tiny “change bundle” that the datacenter firewall team will pick up and run as soon as the maintenance window opens.

What you must deliver
---------------------
1.  A directory called `/home/user/firewall_logs`
2.  Inside that directory, a plain-text file named `firewall_commands.log` that contains the exact firewall commands the team must run.
3.  In your home directory, an executable bash script named `/home/user/firewall_setup.sh` that contains the same commands and starts with a proper shebang.

Exact text that must appear in `firewall_commands.log`
----------------------------------------------------
The file must contain only the following lines in this exact order, with no leading/trailing spaces and each line terminated by a UNIX newline (LF):

ufw allow 2049/tcp comment 'Allow NFS service'
ufw allow 3260/tcp comment 'Allow iSCSI service'
ufw reload

The automated test will fail if:
*   There is any deviation in spelling, capitalisation, spacing, quoting or line-order.
*   A blank line is present before, between or after the required lines.

Requirements for `/home/user/firewall_setup.sh`
---------------------------------------------
*   Must be executable (`chmod 755`).
*   First line must be the shebang: `#!/bin/bash`
*   The shebang must be immediately followed by the same ufw commands shown above, in the same order.
*   No additional commands, comments or blank lines are allowed.

Summary checklist
-----------------
*   Create `/home/user/firewall_logs` (if it does not exist).
*   Place `firewall_commands.log` inside it with the lines shown above.
*   Create `/home/user/firewall_setup.sh` at the top of your home directory with the shebang plus the required commands.
*   Ensure `/home/user/firewall_setup.sh` is executable.

You are acting as a backup-operator who has been asked to activate and test the “restore_test” job that already exists in the local configuration files.  
All work must be done from a regular shell under `/home/user`.

**PRE-EXISTING MATERIAL**  
1. A YAML configuration file:  
   `/home/user/backup/config.yml`  
2. A TOML job definition file:  
   `/home/user/backup/job.toml`  

**GOAL**  

**YAML changes**  
* Inside `/home/user/backup/config.yml` set `jobs.restore_test.enabled` to `true`  
* Change `jobs.restore_test.destination` to `/restores/test`  
* Change `jobs.restore_test.retention_days` to `3`  

**TOML changes**  
* In `/home/user/backup/job.toml`  
   * Set `[general].log_level` to `"DEBUG"`  
   * Set `[general].threads` to `4`  
   * Under `[job.restore_test]`  
     * `cron` must be `"30 3 * * 1-5"`  
     * `compression` must be `"xz"`  

**Logging requirement (MANDATORY)**  
* Create the directory `/home/user/backup/restore_test` (if it does not already exist).  
* Inside that directory create a plain-text log file named `restore_steps.log` in exactly this three-line format (no extra blank lines, trailing spaces or different timestamps):

```
2024-01-01 00:00:00 - STEP - Created restore directory
2024-01-01 00:00:01 - STEP - Updated YAML configuration
2024-01-01 00:00:02 - STEP - Updated TOML configuration
```

**VERIFICATION CHECKS PERFORMED BY THE GRADER**  
* The YAML file must exist and contain all keys exactly as specified, white-space insensitive but value-exact.  
* The TOML file must exist with the edited keys and values exactly as specified (quotation marks and spacing must follow TOML syntax).  
* `/home/user/backup/restore_test/restore_steps.log` must exist, be readable by the current user and contain exactly the three lines shown above, in that order, newline-terminated.  
* All paths and filenames are case-sensitive.

No additional output files, temporary files or directories are allowed anywhere under `/home/user`.  
If any requirement is unclear, assume the stricter interpretation.

You are acting as a junior network-operations engineer who has been handed a
raw ping-output capture and asked to prepare a concise connectivity report for
your lead.  
A text file already exists at  

    /home/user/network_raw.log  

It contains three completely logged ping sessions (Google DNS 8.8.8.8,
Cloudflare DNS 1.1.1.1 and example.com).  
Your assignment is to distill the key statistics from that raw material and
save the results in a brand-new log file whose format can be checked by an
automated test.

What you must deliver
---------------------
1.  Make sure the directory  

        /home/user/network_diagnostics  

    exists (create it if it does not).  
2.  Inside that directory create a file named  

        connection_summary.log  

3.  The file must contain exactly four lines, each terminated by a single UNIX
    newline (`\n`).  Use the *pipe* character (`|`) as the sole field separator
    and **do not add extra whitespace before or after any field**.  
4.  Line-by-line content specification:

    a.  Line 1 – header (fixed literal text):
        `Host|Tx|Rx|Loss%|Avg_RTT_ms`

    b.  Line 2 – statistics for 8.8.8.8
    c.  Line 3 – statistics for 1.1.1.1
    d.  Line 4 – statistics for example.com

    The data lines (2-4) must appear in that exact order and follow this exact
    pattern:

        `<host>|<packets-transmitted>|<packets-received>|<packet-loss-percent>|<average-rtt>`

    *   For hosts that achieved **100 %** packet loss, write `N/A` (all caps) in
        the Avg_RTT_ms field.  
    *   All numeric RTT values must retain three decimal places exactly as found
        in the raw log.

    Example of the required final file:

        Host|Tx|Rx|Loss%|Avg_RTT_ms
        8.8.8.8|4|4|0|14.235
        1.1.1.1|4|4|0|30.312
        example.com|4|0|100|N/A

5.  When you are finished, display the contents of the resulting
    /home/user/network_diagnostics/connection_summary.log so that its contents
    are visible in the console; the grader will look for this exact output.

You are an integration-backend developer.  
Inside `/home/user/api_test/logs` you already have a single file called `access.log` that contains raw traffic traces captured from your staging API gateway.  
Your objective is to prepare three different artefacts:

1.  A POST-error extract:  
    *   Keep the **exact** same column order as the original log.  
    *   Include only those lines whose HTTP method is “POST” **and** whose status code is 500 or higher.  
    *   Save the result to `/home/user/api_test/output/critical_posts.log`.

2.  A per-endpoint statistics CSV:  
    *   One line per distinct endpoint (column 4 of the log).  
    *   Columns, in order, separated by plain commas (no spaces):  
        `endpoint,total_requests,avg_latency_ms,max_latency_ms,5xx_count`  
    *   The first line must be the header shown above.  
    *   The average latency must be arithmetically correct and may contain “.5”; do **not** round to whole numbers except where the arithmetic result is already an integer.  
    *   The file must be named `/home/user/api_test/output/endpoint_summary.csv`.

3.  A privacy-sanitised log:  
    *   Replace the entire IP (“column 2”) of every line with the literal string `REDACTED`.  
    *   All other content and ordering must stay untouched.  
    *   Save this as `/home/user/api_test/output/access_sanitized.log`.

Create the directory `/home/user/api_test/output` if it is missing.  
When you are done, list the three new files so the tester can see they exist.  
Do **not** delete or modify the original `/home/user/api_test/logs/access.log`.

The automated tests will confirm:
*   The three files exist at the exact paths given.  
*   `critical_posts.log` contains only the qualifying POST 5xx lines, in their original order.  
*   `endpoint_summary.csv` has the exact header and four data rows with correct calculated values.  
*   `access_sanitized.log` is byte-for-byte identical to the original log except that every IP field is replaced by `REDACTED`.
